title: Function Notation and Mini Performance Task
module: Week 1 - Linear Equations, Inequalities, and Functions
learningTarget: I can evaluate function notation and use a linear model to answer a real question.
whyItMatters: Function notation looks fancy, but it is mostly input and output. CAASPP uses it because it is a compact way to ask what happens when a real situation changes.

sections.title: Quick Start
sections.studentText: For f(x) = 4x + 9, the input is x and the output is f(x).

sections.title: Watch: Evaluating discrete functions
sections.watchFor: Watch how the input points to exactly one output.

contextQuestions.prompt: When you see f(4), what number is the input?
contextQuestions.feedback: The input is the number inside the parentheses.

contextQuestions.prompt: Why is function notation useful when a problem has many possible inputs?
contextQuestions.feedback: A strong answer says function notation clearly shows which input is being used.

sections.title: Input, Rule, Output
sections.studentText: Function notation is a machine label. If f(x) = 4x + 9, then f(3) means use 3 as the input. The output is 21.

workedExample.prompt: A school dance has a setup cost of $180 and earns $8 per ticket. Let P(t) = 8t - 180 be the profit after selling t tickets.
steps: After 50 tickets, the event has a $220 profit.
workedExample.commonMistake: Do not stop at 220 without units or meaning. In context, 220 means dollars of profit.

sections.title: Mini Performance Task: School Dance Budget
sections.scenario: Student Council is planning a dance. Renting the room and equipment costs $180. Tickets sell for $8 each. The profit model is P(t) = 8t - 180.

questions.prompt: How many tickets must be sold to break even?
questions.feedback: Solve 8t - 180 >= 0. The exact break-even is 22.5 tickets, so the council must sell at least 23 whole tickets.

questions.prompt: Explain why 22.5 tickets is not a practical final answer in this situation.
questions.feedback: A strong answer says tickets are whole items, so the council must round up to 23 tickets to avoid losing money.
The model starts at -180 because the event has a $180 cost before ticket sales.

questions.prompt: Student Council wants at least $500 profit. Write an inequality and explain how many tickets they need to sell.
questions.feedback: A strong answer writes 8t - 180 >= 500 and solves to t >= 85.

gate.completion: Student attempts the video questions, mini performance task, and Week 1 check.
gate.mastery: At least 80% objective accuracy and 8/10 or better on the modeling rubric.
gate.remediation: Review input/output notation and solve one break-even problem with a smaller number set.
gate.enrichment: Change the ticket price and explain how the break-even point changes.
